spotlight: The Beijing summit dominates the day: Trump flattered, Xi held firm, and Asian allies are rattled by talk of trading away Taiwan arms sales. The Gulf war keeps reshaping markets — oil inventories plunging, energy rationing fears in Germany, and a UN food-security warning. At home, the government weighs cheaper electricity but balks at diesel tax cuts, while school reform and ministerial turbulence run through Czech politics.

stories.title: Government weighs cheaper electricity, rules out diesel tax cut

body.text: Deputy PM and industry minister Karel Havlíček (ANO) does not intend to lower the excise duty on diesel, but is considering relief in the regulated component of the electricity price
. In a separate interview, Havlíček outlined the search for a mechanism to ensure enough gas for next winter, the filling of storage facilities, the renationalisation of ČEZ and the US LNG contract
. The move comes against a tense energy backdrop, with the Gulf war disrupting supply and pushing prices up across Europe.

stories.title: IEA warns of further oil price spikes as inventories plunge

body.text: The International Energy Agency is warning of further 'price spikes' as global oil inventories drop at a record pace
. The pressure is rippling outward: Turkey's central bank lifted its inflation target as the war in the Gulf adds to economic pain
. Germany, meanwhile, fears its first energy rationing this summer because of gas-supply instability following the conflict
. The squeeze is also pushing Europe's chemicals industry toward breaking point, hit by high energy costs, Chinese competition and the war's fallout

stories.title: UN warns of food crisis as Gulf war disrupts fertiliser supply

body.text: The conflict in the Gulf has disrupted supplies of fertiliser and energy, and the UN is warning that millions of people could be left without food
. Russia and the US are profiting from the resulting rise in prices
. In the US, a separate effect is already visible: a cut to welfare benefits has started to bite into the grocery sales of large food chains

stories.title: Wave of layoffs hits German industry

body.text: VW, BioNTech and other German firms are announcing a wave of layoffs, and analysts fear it is only the beginning

. The mood in corporate Germany contrasts with bright spots in European tech, where investors are chasing the continent's few AI plays such as STMicroelectronics, Aixtron and Nokia, whose shares have soared this year as the US tech frenzy goes global
. Swedish fintech Klarna, meanwhile, broke even for the first time since its blockbuster listing

stories.title: Schillerová unveils new Republic Bonds issue

body.text: Finance minister Alena Schillerová has presented a new issue of Republic Bonds aimed at retail investors, giving people another chance to lend their money to the state

. The issuance coincides with a broader fiscal pick-up: after the president signed the budget law, contract signings worth up to CZK 19 billion are getting under way at ŘSD and the Railway Administration for roads and railways
. Municipalities, too, have started investing again after 15 years of hoarding surpluses, with investment spending up by a quarter ahead of local elections

stories.title: Italians buy Cerva Group, creating a CZK 12bn company

body.text: Italian workwear manufacturer U-Power has completed its acquisition of the Czech company Cerva Group, with the combined turnover reaching over CZK 12 billion
. The deal is one of several cross-border moves involving Czech players: Czech investors have closed another billion-crown transaction in Poland, acquiring an established shopping centre

stories.title: Indebted Solek presents new reorganisation plan

body.text: The solar group Solek has presented an updated reorganisation plan offering lower repayment to creditors after a new valuation of its assets at CZK 83 million
. The revised terms reflect the steep markdown in the company's underlying value and underscore the strain on the indebted developer.

stories.title: Trump flatters, Xi holds firm at Beijing summit over Taiwan

body.text: Xi Jinping warned Trump that the 'Taiwan question' is a critical point in US-China relations as the Beijing summit opened
. Trump's stated plan to discuss US arms sales to Taiwan with Xi has rattled Asian allies
. The contrast in style was striking: the NYT noted that Trump was flattering while Xi was resolute, a difference that spoke volumes

stories.title: Gulf monarchies press Trump to disarm Iran militarily

body.text: Saudi Arabia, the UAE, Bahrain and Qatar want the US-Israeli operation to leave Iran militarily weakened
. At the same time, Saudi Arabia is floating a non-aggression pact with Tehran in response to the operation
. The NYT reports a covert dimension: US officials say Saudi Arabia and the UAE carried out secret attacks inside Iran
. Lebanon and Israel, meanwhile, are heading to Washington for a third round of direct talks as their ceasefire nears its end

stories.title: Ukraine cool on US peace talks, braces for Russian spring offensive

body.text: Neither Russia nor Ukraine believes in a revival of US-brokered peace talks even after the Middle East war ends
. Analysts warn that once nature turns green, the Russians will attack again, while Ukraine is hitting back with hundreds of drones deep inside Russia, striking the Primorsk oil terminal
. Kyiv is also trying to cut its dependence on US technology: arms maker Fire Point is launching its own satellites

stories.title: US abruptly cancels deployment of 4,000 troops to Poland

body.text: The Pentagon has cancelled the planned deployment of 4,000 American troops to Poland, causing concern in Europe
. The move sharpens questions about Washington's commitment to the alliance just as NATO foreign ministers gather in Sweden, where all eyes are on Marco Rubio over the US position

stories.title: Plaga admits cuts to school programmes failed, reform slips

body.text: The new education minister Plaga (ANO) has admitted that the radical cull of secondary-school programmes did not work and the reform is being postponed by a year
. Separately, the education ministry presented a reform of higher education that would weaken academic senates, strengthen the powers of rectors and create a new university board

stories.title: More turbulence at Juchelka's ministry as deputy resigns

body.text: There is further upheaval at minister Juchelka's department, where a deputy minister has resigned and a second adviser — dubbed 'Mr Indispensable' — has lost his post
. The reshuffle adds to the impression of instability at the top of the ministry.

stories.title: Audit finds nonsensical subsidies for road repairs

body.text: The Supreme Audit Office found that more than half of the subsidies from the Ministry for Regional Development for repairing municipal roads went to insignificant local roads
. The findings raise fresh questions about how regional-development money is allocated and overseen.

stories.title: Bank results once again fail to disappoint
stories.author: Karel Nedvěd

body.text: According to Fio banka analyst Karel Nedvěd, the fourth-quarter 2025 results of Erste, Komerční banka and Moneta were solid and offer a measure of certainty in turbulent times
. He frames the sector's steady performance as a reassuring anchor amid wider geopolitical and market uncertainty.

stories.title: Are we facing another inflation spike?
stories.author: Julie Hrstková

body.text: Julie Hrstková writes about the risks of a new inflation spike linked to rising oil prices after the war in the Gulf
. She frames it as a possible 'return to the past', warning that the energy shock could feed back into consumer prices.

stories.title: Czech economy grew three times faster than Slovakia's
stories.author: Anna Šuhajdová

body.text: Anna Šuhajdová compares the economic growth of the Czech Republic (2.6%) and Slovakia (0.8%), arguing the divergence will deepen further this year
. She sees structural differences between the two economies driving the widening gap.

stories.title: Prague's office market: building has stalled
stories.author: Pavel Kadera

body.text: Pavel Kadera writes that Prague's office market is experiencing a slowdown in new construction, driven by two trends
. Developers are being drawn toward residential projects, while offices now pay off mainly in central locations.

stories.title: Investment bubbles are a normal feature of markets
stories.author: Michal Stupavský

body.text: Conseq investment strategist Michal Stupavský examines theories of investment bubbles as described by Grantham, Shiller, Soros and Buffett
. His central theme is the return of valuations to their long-run average.

stories.title: Are fears over corporate debt justified?
stories.author: Jiří Król

body.text: Jiří Król argues that worries about the private-credit market — over the valuation of portfolios and the impact of AI on the software sector — are exaggerated
. His view contrasts with a more cautious FAZ analysis of private credit, which flags rising fund withdrawals, valuation questions and AI-related risks

stories.title: Slavia in turmoil amid political pressure

body.text: An analysis of the situation at football club Slavia argues that Tykač's club faces political pressure after a controversial derby, with the 'unsinkable' Tvrdík at the centre of events
. The commentary captures the heated atmosphere around the club following the chaotic match.
